s2.q: During pregnancy, cardiac output increases due to:
options: Decreased stroke volume and decreased heart rate
Increased stroke volume and increased heart rate
s2.explain: Cardiac output increases due to increased stroke volume and increased heart rate. There is also reduced systemic vascular resistance. Cardiac output rises ~30-50% by the third trimester.

s2.q: Cardiac decompensation during pregnancy most commonly occurs at:
options: 28-32 weeks, during labor, and immediately postpartum
s2.explain: Cardiac decompensation most often occurs at 28-32 weeks (peak blood volume), during labor, and immediately postpartum (autotransfusion from contracted uterus) — when blood volume and venous return are at their highest.

s2.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s2.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
